  • Cycle data was always aligned with strategy configuration
  • No dependency on LTMC or LSMW was required
  • Manual creation effort was eliminated

The solution proved to be stable, reusable, and fully controlled.

CONCLUSION

MPLAN_CREATE becomes a practical and powerful alternative when standard migration tools do not support complex PM scenarios such as Multiple Counter Maintenance Plans with cycle sets. By deriving cycle values from the table T351P and counter points from the table IMPTT, we were able to automate the creation of upload program for Multiple Counter Maintenance Plan, while keeping the logic aligned with SAP configuration. This approach helped us to bridge the gap between functional configuration and technical automation in a clean and maintainable way.

entry.title: Replicate SAP S/4HANA Measuring Points and Measurement Documents to SAP Field Service Management
entry.content:
In asset-intensive industries, equipment health isn’t just a metric—it’s the backbone of operational efficiency. Organizations running SAP S/4HANA often use Measuring Points and Measurement Documents to monitor equipment performance and condition. These data points help ensure timely maintenance and prevent costly downtime.

But here’s the challenge:
When field technicians work in SAP Field Service Management (FSM), they often lack access to these critical insights. Without real-time data in the field, service teams face delays, reactive maintenance, and frustrated customers.

So, what’s the solution?
Seamless integration between SAP S/4HANA and SAP FSM. By replicating Measuring Points and Measurement Documents from S/4HANA to FSM, technicians gain real-time visibility into asset health—right where the work happens.

Why Does This Integration Matter?

Imagine this scenario:

  • A technician is dispatched to repair a pump.
  • In FSM, they see the service order but have no clue about the pump’s recent performance trends.
  • They spend extra time diagnosing issues that could have been avoided if they had access to measurement readings.

With integration:

  • The technician sees the latest pressure readings, temperature and vibration trends in FSM.
  • They arrive prepared, diagnose faster, and fix proactively.
  • Result? Less downtime, happier customers, and lower costs.

Key Benefits of Integration

  • Real-time synchronization of equipment performance data between S/4HANA and FSM.
  • Improved visibility for technicians in the field.
  • Proactive service delivery, leading to higher customer satisfaction.

Scope of the Integration

Here’s what’s included in the S/4HANA On-Premise October 2025 release.

  • Measuring Point Replication
    • Create or update measuring points in S/4HANA.
    • Changes automatically replicate to FSM for the corresponding equipment.
  • Measurement Document Replication
    • Measurement readings or counters created in S/4HANA replicate to FSM.
    • Readings can also be captured during maintenance order confirmation.
    • Measurement readings captured in FSM replicate back to S/4HANA is available via SAP Note: 3684637 - Implementation of Replication Logic for Measurement Document from SAP Field Service Management to SAP S/4HANA
  • Initial and Delta Loads
    • Supports both bulk initial load and incremental updates.
  • Multi-company Support
    • Data replication can be configured for multiple FSM companies.

Prerequisites

  • For Measuring Points in FSM:
    • Measurement Point Category (handled by integration).
    • Equipment and Characteristics (already managed via MAIF framework).
  • For Measurement Documents in FSM:
    • The corresponding Measuring Point must exist in FSM.

Configuration Steps

Delta Replication

  • For single FSM company:
    Navigate to SPRO → Service → Integration with Other SAP Solutions → SAP Field Service Management → Configure Integration

Maintain following settings (Use the same RFC destination as Equipment replication.):

 

  • For multiple FSM companies:
    SPRO → Service → Integration with Other SAP Solutions → SAP Field Service Management → Configure Multiple Company Replication

Define S/4HANA FSM Company:

 

Assig FSM Company Details:

 

 

Assign Replication Object Details:

 

  • Create BGRFC inbound destination:
    Transaction: SBGRFCCONF

 

 

  • Extract AIF Contents:
    Transaction: /AIF/CONTENT_EXTRACT

 

 

Initial Load

  • Go to DRFIMG → Define Custom Settings for Data Replication → Define Technical Settings for Business Systems.

 

Maintain Business System and Logical System (Ignore if already created):

 

Define Business Systems, BOs:

 

Define Business Systems, Bos, Communication Channel:

 

Maintain the above settings for BO Type 5236 as well.

 

  • Define Replication Models:

Go to DRFIMG -> Define Custom Settings for Data Replication -> Define Replication Models

 

Create following replication models:

 

             

Maintain the following settings for Replication Model MDOC_REPL:

 

 

 

 

 

 

Maintain the abovementioned settings for Replication Model MP_REPL as well.

 

Delta replication Demo

Scenario: Wind energy operator: Each turbine has measuring point for running hours. This points (created on the equipment/functional location in S/4HANA) is replicate to FSM. When maintenance technician performs the service then maintenance measurement readings are captured during maintenance order confirmation. These readings are also replicated to FSM.

Create Measuring Point: tcode: IK01

 

 

Check the measuring point replication log in S/4HANA

Tcode /AIF/ERR

 

 

 

Check the replication in FSM:

Note: Measuring Points and Measurement documents can be viewed with in the equipment using Mobile App. At present it cannot be viewed via FSM website.

 

 

Create a maintenance order for the maintenance of the Wind Turbine and assign the Measuring point as PRT

 

 

Create maintenance order confirmation using tcode: IW41 and create measurement document

 

 

Measurement document can also be viewed in tcode IK13

 

 

Check the measurement document replication log in S/4HANA

Tcode /AIF/ERR

 

 

 

For more details, visit https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_ON-PREMISE/738a456365c0414faba6426d05fd8674/0ccd50bd60554bfe90af11864497f5de.html?locale=en-US.

Final Thoughts

Integrating Measuring Points and Measurement Documents between SAP S/4HANA and FSM isn’t just a technical upgrade—it’s a strategic move toward data-driven field service. With real-time insights, technicians can work smarter, customers stay happier, and businesses save money.

entry.title: SAP Build Process Automation(SBPA):S4HANA Asset Management-Automating Major Maintenance Work Request
entry.content:
This blog provides a broad outline of how SAP Business Process Automation can help maintenance teams streamline the "Major Maintenance Work" Request Form, including the Sanction Committee Governance approval process.

What is SAP Build Process Automation?

SAP Build Process Automation (SBPA) is a low-code, no-code service on the SAP BTP (Business Technology Platform) that enables users to automate, manage, and optimize business processes and workflows without writing code.

We can start the business process in four ways: using a request form or an API or an Event or a Scheduled Trigger.

 

My Business Use Case:

My maintenance team is preparing for a major equipment overhaul. They must present the business case with the supporting evidence to the Sanction Committee Governance for FY26 budget approval. Once the sanction committee approves the budget, the maintenance team initiates the maintenance order process.

In this blog post, I will outline the process of creating the major maintenance work request form and explain how the approval/rejection workflow operates. (Note: The automatic creation of maintenance order at the end of the approval process will be outlined in my next blog post).

For my business use case in this blog post, the process is triggered when a form is submitted. The process then continues with a combination of steps, such as Approval Forms, Email notifications until it reaches its end.

 

If you want to gain insights into how SAP JOULE skill works, please check out my latest blog posts below.

  • SAP JOULE & ME : My First JOULE Skill in SAP EHSM-Incident Management Without Coding 
  • SAP Joule Skill for Technical Completion of Maintenance Orders in S/4HANA Asset Management 

 

We will first create a business process project using SAP Build Lobby. Then, we will design the business process. Once the business process is completed, we will create and configure forms. After that, we will create and configure approval form, and finally, we will configure the email notification.

Once we complete the setup, the final result looks like this.

Let us first create a project using SAP Build Process Automation.

Business process projects are a collection of skills in SAP Build Process Automation. Projects are part of the internal business processes of a company and are defined based on business scenarios. A project can contain a set of processes, forms, automations and decisions, which are reusable artifacts.

 

Inside a project, we can create a process. We can create a business process version using a combination of artifacts (such as forms and decisions) and process controls (such as branches, conditions, and Mail Notifications).

Let us now add forms to the business process. For Instance let us use "Form" to trigger the business process.

Let us design the form using "Open Editor".

for instance, let us add Input fields with labels.

  • General Information: we can add the dropdown values wherever required, so that the maintenance team can select the desired value while submitting the form.

  • Scope Of Work

  • Cost Estimate : Projected Cost for Materials and Services

Now the trigger form is ready. Let us design the approval process form.

The approval form is used to get faster and easier approvals from the business users. These forms are converted into tasks in an automated workflow which will appear in the My Inbox of the user. We can design and modify without coding.

Let us design the Approval Form in the form builder by dragging and dropping fields into the form editor and configure the field settings. As the L1 Approver does not have to modify the data, let us make the fields "Read Only" for the L1 approver and add a new field to input L1 Approver Comments.

 

Now, let us configure the L1 Approval form and map the fields. We can use bind list wherever required. The subject that we mention in general section will be displayed when this task appears in "My Inbox". And this task will be appeared in the "My Inbox" of the provided "Recipient".

Similarly, let us create an L2 Approver Form. Let us use the option "Duplicate".

Duplicate feature is used to copy artifacts within the business process. The copy feature is used to copy artifacts across the business process. 

Similarly, let us configure the general information and map the input fields.

We have just completed the approval form for the major maintenance work request process.

My organization demands an email notification to be sent to the requestor whenever there is an action, i.e., Approve or Rejection happens on the Major Maintenance Work Request Form.

Let us configure the Header and Subject of the E-Mail for the "Rejection" process.

Now, let us configure the E-Mail Body.

Similarly, let us configure the Header, Subject and Body of the E-Mail Notification for the "Approval" process.

Once the Major Maintenance Work Request Form with two levels of Approvals  is completed, we can release and deploy it in the respective environment.

Now, Let us run the business process...

Business Case Details:

Scope Of Work Details:

Cost Estimate Details:

Now let us submit the form. 

The L1 Approver can now check "My Inbox"  to review the Major Maintenance Work Request Form.

Since the L1 Approver Comments are mandatory for this form, the option "Approve" or "Rejection" will be highlighted once L1 Approver provides the comments.

Once the Major Maintenance Request Form is approved by the L1 Approver, It will be displayed in L2 Approver`s Inbox. Let us approve it and send an email notification to the requestor upon the approval. L1 Approver comments are displayed while L2 Approver approves or rejects the form.

Once the workflow is ended, we can monitor the processes.

The monitoring page collects the events coming from the deployed workflows and automation jobs and present them to the user. These events represents the status of the jobs that have been run, and the business activity of the monitoring events.

For my business use case, we can view the process, workflow status using "Process and Workflow Instances" application.

😊This brings me to the end of process walkthrough on SAP Build Process Automation using forms as a trigger for one of the use cases, i.e., Major Maintenance Work Request.

In Summary,  we have designed and configured a digital form to capture structured data for a "Major Maintenance Work". Implemented multi level approvals (L1- L2). And also triggered automated email notifications to the requestor, improving transparency and reducing manual efforts. This entire approach established a foundation that can be scaled up and showed how automation can reduce repetitive tasks and speeds up the approval cycle with more transparency.

Stay tuned for more updates on SAP Build Process Automation and SAP Joule Skills/Agents in S/4HANA Asset Management Space...

-- 

Yours Sincerely,

Pardhasaradhi Reddy. C

entry.title: Automating Price Updates from MM Contracts into Task Lists (SAP PM): A Pragmatic Deep Dive
entry.content:
Business Problem

Maintenance organizations often run recurring plan/task list operations with service lines (ESLL). Prices are maintained centrally in MM service contracts (EKPO/ESLH/ESLL + PRS condition). Without automation, updating those prices across all task lists is a manual grind — and highly error‑prone.

Requirement: Get current contract prices into task list service lines without manually touching every ESLL row.

Solution: The report reads current PRS conditions (A081 → KONP) per LV leaf from the contract structure and updates the corresponding ESLL price rows in the maintenance task list — deterministically mapped via EXTROW.




What the Report Does (Functional Flow)

1) Discover the Contract’s LV Leaves

  • Resolve the root PACKNO from EKPO (PSTYP = 9).
  • Traverse the ESLL hierarchy using SUB_PACKNO and collect only leaf rows (non‑packages).
  • Extract for each leaf: PACKNO, INTROW, EXTROW, SRVPOS, KTEXT1.

Why leaves?
They’re the price‑bearing service lines that map 1:1 into the task list.

2) Read Current Conditions from A081/KONP

  • For each leaf, pull A081 using (KAPPL, KSCHL, KONT_PACK, KONT_ZEILE, WERKS, DATBI).
  • From KONP, read:
    • KBETR (unit price)
    • KONWA (currency)
    • KPEIN (price unit)
    • KMEIN (price unit UoM)

Build an internal price table keyed by EXTROW for fast lookup via binary search.

3) Load the Task List ESLL Rows

  • Use PLAS/PLPO → ESLH/ESLL (or MPOS as fallback) to fetch all relevant ESLL rows.
  • Bring currency context from ESLH-WAERS.
  • Optionally restrict by operation number VORNR.

4) Update Prices Consistently

Join logic

Match contract prices to task list lines via EXTROW.

Guardrails

  • Currency must match (KONWA == ESLH-WAERS).
  • PEINH and MENGE must be present.
  • If required data is missing → skip safely.

  • Pricing math

    NETWR/BRTWR = KBETR * (MENGE / PEINH)

    • Optional single price (TBTWR)

      Set only if units match (KMEIN == MEINS

      TBTWR = KBETR * (ESLL-PEINH / KONP-KPEIN)

      Optional MPOS header price

      MPOS-NETPR = KBETR

  • Modes

    • Simulation (default): no DB writes, full trace.
    • Real run: commits changes and summarizes results.

 

 

Why EXTROW Is the Right Mapping Key

  • Stability: More robust than internal row numbers when LV structures change.
  • Clarity: Business and IT can rely on one rule:
    “If EXTROW is copied from contract to task list, prices map 1:1.”
  • Performance: Enables sorting + binary search instead of fuzzy matching.



Key Technical Elements:

PARAMETERS: p_ebeln TYPE ekko-ebeln OBLIGATORY,   " Contract number
            p_ebelp TYPE ekpo-ebelp,              " Optional item
            p_werks TYPE werks_d    OBLIGATORY,   " Plant for A081
            p_kschl TYPE konp-kschl DEFAULT 'PRS',
            p_kappl TYPE konh-kappl DEFAULT 'MS'.

PARAMETERS: p_plnty TYPE plko-plnty DEFAULT 'A' OBLIGATORY, " Task list type
            p_plnnr TYPE plko-plnnr OBLIGATORY,             " Group
            p_plnal TYPE plko-plnal OBLIGATORY,             " Group counter
            p_vornr TYPE plpo-vornr.                        " Optional op

PARAMETERS: p_test   AS CHECKBOX DEFAULT 'X', " Simulation
            p_dbg    AS CHECKBOX DEFAULT 'X', " Debug/trace
            p_setmp  AS CHECKBOX,             " Set MPOS-NETPR
            p_settbw AS CHECKBOX DEFAULT 'X'. " Set ESLL-TBTWR​

 

Core Calculations

lv_scale     = gs_out_tl-menge / gs_out_tl-peinh.
lv_new_netwr = ls_price-kbetr * lv_scale.     " ESLL-NETWR/BRTWR

" Optional TBTWR (single price), only if units align:
IF ls_price-kmein = gs_out_tl-meins.
  lv_factor     = (gs_out_tl-peinh / ls_price-kpein) OR 1 if KPEIN = 0
  lv_tbtwr_new  = ls_price-kbetr * lv_factor.
ENDIF.

 

  • Built‑In Safeguards

    • Currency check (avoids accidental conversions).
    • Quantity/unit checks (avoid division by zero).
    • Unit congruence enforced for TBTWR.
    • Simulation mode gives trace + predictable dry-runs.
    • Counters summarize:
      • found leaves
      • A081 hits
      • KONP reads
      • task list lines
      • updates vs. skips
      • MPOS/TBTWR writes



Conclusion

The report solves a high‑friction maintenance problem with a precise, auditable mechanism:
it maps current contract prices into task list service lines using a stable external key (EXTROW), respects unit/currency safeguards, and allows safe simulation-first execution.

The full ABAP code can be found on my github: https://github.com/ProjectNautilus001/ABAP-Solutions-for-SAP-PM/blob/main/Y_PM_WPLAN_PREISUPDATE

entry.title: New in SAP EAM with S/4HANA 2025 FPS01: Task Planning Management API
entry.content:
Task Planning Management in S/4HANA Asset Management - SAP Media Share

 

Hello EAM community!

I’m Michael Spiess, team architect and scrum master of the Wall/E team. I’m excited to share our team’s first S/4HANA delivery — and it’s a big one!

What’s new:

  • Task Planning Management API: Programmatic access to roughly 90% of Compatible Unit Management capabilities. 
  • New feature for the Process Maintenance Order app (W0017). You can now assign a compatible unit design to a maintenance order. 

Enjoy exploring the new API, and trying out the app enhancement, and stay tuned — more updates from the Wall/E team are on the way!

 

What is Task Planning Management?

Task Planning Management (TPM) is the successor of Compatible Unit Management.

The primary reason for this rebranding is to emphasize that the compatible unit functionality will be enhanced to serve not only the utility industry but also other sectors.

These enhancements are planned for future releases; in the current release, the new term is only applicable to the APIs. 

 

What is Compatible Unit Management?

Compatible Unit Management is an enterprise extension in SAP EAM for organizations that construct and maintain physical assets using standard, repeatable units of work. With compatible units (CUs), you define a reusable work package for an asset or asset component and enrich it with job-specific details, such as:

  • Job steps and planned duration
  • Crew and skill requirements
  • Special tools and materials
  • Reference documents
  • Accounting and cost assignments

CUs are modular by design and typically mirror the engineering structure (EBOM) of an asset. They’re often authored in CAD systems and then transferred to SAP EAM to bring the CU design to life — from resource allocation for execution through to financial settlement.

For example, one CU might define a utility pole, and another might define the installation of crossarms on that pole. Multiple CUs can be bundled into a CU design document to represent the entire project, for example to build a utility connection for a new construction site.  In a planning phase you may be interested to discuss design variants before you decide together with the customer for the best option. This is why you can assign multiple design documents to a construction measure document and release the selected design document from there. The construction measure can be cross-linked with a WBS element, a notification, or a master maintenance order.

 

How do Task Planning Management and Compatible Unit Management relate to each other?

The new Task Planning Management API creates and updates compatible unit master data and has full access to the two documents in Compatible Unit Management:

  • Compatible Unit Design
  • Construction Measure

This diagram shows how the new business objects of Task Planning Management - built with latest RAP (ABAP RESTful Application Programming Model) technology -  get full access to master data and documents from databases of the Compatible Unit domain.

Figure 1: Relationship between Task Planning Management and Compatible Unit Management

 

 

End users can still use Compatible Unit Management in SAP GUI while developers can already use the new business objects to access Compatible Unit Management functionality from API version 1.0.0 onwards.

 

What are the benefits of Task Planning Management?

 

Shorter design cycles

The Task Planning Management API streamlines the conversion of an engineering bill of material (EBOM) into a task planning design document which represents the  service bill of material (SBOM). CAD developers can easily consume the TPM OData V4 API as a web-extension. This to offers engineers  a seamless app experience, enabling them to create a task planning design document on the fly while working on the engineering bill of material.  Once the engineering design document is released, the task planning design document is simultaneously released. This becomes a single, reusable source of truth that maintenance planners can embed directly in maintenance orders for resource planning, execution, maintenance and financial settlement.

The following diagram shows  in three detail levels how CAD objects are mapped to the corresponding business objects of Task Planning Management:

Figure 2: EBOM to SBOM transformation example with Task Planning Management API

Enhanced work order efficiency

Whether you assign a task planning element to a reusable design document or ad-hoc to a maintenance order, selecting the right operations for each part of the build becomes straightforward. Thanks to a modular structure and inheritance from the parent planning element, data entry is minimal. In the following example the user inputs two values ("install", "classic") to estimate time and costs for a classic pole installation:

Figure 3: classification system and multi-level value inheritance boosts work efficiency

More accurate job estimates

When Operation Account Assignment (OAA) is enabled, you can track actuals down to each task planning element. This granular visibility allows teams to fine-tune planned costs for specific planning elements, resulting in more accurate quotes and better cost predictability across projects.

 

How will Task Planning Management evolve in the future?

Let’s take a look ahead! Please keep in mind that these are my personal views. For the most reliable view of what’s coming, explore SAP Road Maps at https://roadmaps.sap.com/ and search for “EAM.”

One of the hottest topics I see is the integration of Task Planning Management with SAP S/4HANA Service Management. Having a task planning design for execution and maintenance is fine, but using this design document easily in a quotation process doubles the value of Task Planning Management!

Integration with SAP Project System (PS) is another win. With precise durations and work centers derived from the design, project managers can confidently schedule construction phases and identify the optimal slots in the overall project plan. Bringing in SAP Geographical Enablement Framework (GEF) would be equally powerful. It would allow maintenance planners to search for installed planning elements and designs on a map, accelerating decisions and improving asset insights.

To tie these capabilities together, SAP’s AI assistant, Joule, is the natural companion! With Task Planning Management skills, Joule lets users adjust designs on the fly and guide them straight to the screens where those changes impact quote values and project timelines.

Figure 4: Task Planning Management agent communication

 

How can I try out the API?

 

Prerequisites

  • Business function for Compatible Unit Management switched on
  • Initial configuration for Compatible Unit Management completed
  • Permission to new authorization objects for Task Planning Management (I_TPMTPE, I_TPMDSGN, I_TPMCM) granted
  • Access to transaction /IWFND/GW_CLIENT 

 

Overview about the process - from task planning element via design to the new app feature

Start exploring the Task Planning Management API by creating your first task planning element in a few steps! Once the first task planning element is created, valuated, and released, you can further enhance it to a multi-level task planning element to represent a classic utility pole, like the pole from figure 3. You can use the pole in a new design document to test your first task planning element. To test out all three business objects you can create a construction measure and release the design from there. Use the new feature "Assign CU-Design" in app W0017 "Process Maintenance Order" to verify the successful execution of the request.

Here are the required requests in an overview:

Step

API request

How many times

1.0

Create task planning element 

3x (pole, cross arm, insulator)

1.1

Create value assignment

3x (pole, cross arm, insulator)

1.2

Release task planning element

3x (pole, cross arm, insulator)

1.3

Create task planning element BOM item

2x (pole, cross arm)

2.0

Create task planning design document

1x

2.1

Create the value assignment for the utility pole

1x

3.0

Create construction measure and release design 

1x

 

Step 1.0: Create a task planning element in 7 steps 

Let us create the first task planning element for an 40 ft wooden utility pole:

1.  SAP Logon to the S/4HANA 2025 FPS01 system

2. Call transaction /IWFND/GW_CLIENT

3. Change the radio-button from GET to POST

4. Copy this line into the Request URI text box:

/sap/opu/odata4/sap/api_taskplanningelement/srvd_a2x/sap/taskplanningelement/0001/TaskPlanningElement

5. Copy these lines into the HTTP-Request body box and replace the values with your compatible unit configuration:

{
  "TaskPlanningElementCategory": "1 - stands for internal numbering",
  "BaseUnitofMeasure": "PC",
  "TaskPlanningElementDescription": "utility pole 40 ft wooden",
  "TskPlngElmntPlantForWorkCenter": "Plant for Work Center of TaskListGroup",
  "TaskListGroup": "should be unique for this Task Planning Element",
  "FunctionalLocationLabelName": "FuncLoc or Equipment"
}

6. Execute the request.

7. HTTP 201 returned. Success! You created your first task planning element in just 7 steps. Your screen should look like this:  

Figure 5: Task Planning Element created with SAP Gateway Client

Let's take a closer look into the HTTP response. Copy the task planning identifier 1539025 to your clipboard for the next request.

{
  "@odata.context" : "$metadata#TaskPlanningElement/$entity",
  "@odata.metadataEtag" : "W/\"20260212230942\"",
  "TaskPlanningElementIdentifier" : "1539025",  //use for next request
  "TaskPlanningElementCategory" : "1",
  "TaskListGroup" : "POLE1"
...
}

 

Step 1.1: Create value assignment for task planning element

Define the allowed range of values for the characteristic CU_ACTION. Supply also characteristics with mandatory value assignments like in my example "CU_TYPE".

/sap/opu/odata4/sap/api_taskplanningelement/srvd_a2x/sap/taskplanningelement/0001/TaskPlanningElement/1539025/SAP__self.CreateValueAssignment{
  "_CharcValueComponent": [  {
      "Characteristic": "CU_ACTION",
      "CharcValue": "INSTALL"
    },    {
      "Characteristic": "CU_ACTION",
      "CharcValue": "REMOVE"
    },    {
      "Characteristic": "CU_ACTION",
      "CharcValue": "INSPEC"
    },    {
      "Characteristic": "CU_TYPE",
      "CharcValue": "classic"
    }  ] }

 

Step 1.2: Release task planning element

Release the task planning element:

/sap/opu/odata4/sap/api_taskplanningelement/srvd_a2x/sap/taskplanningelement/0001/TaskPlanningElement/1539025/SAP__self.Release

Repeat steps 1.1 to 1.3 to create and release the planning elements for the cross arm and the insulator. In my example it is task planning element id 1539027 for the cross arm and 1539028 for the insulator.

 

Step 1.3: Create task planning element BOM item

To reflect the engineering structure of the utility pole, define the pole as the root and the two cross arm as the first children:

/sap/opu/odata4/sap/api_taskplanningelement/srvd_a2x/sap/taskplanningelement/0001/TaskPlanningElement/1539025/_TskPlngElmntBOMItem
{
  "MaintOrdOperationExecutionRate": 1,      //execution factor for operation processing
  "BillOfMaterialComponent": "1539027",     // cross arm
  "NmbrOfCompatibleUnitInstances" : "2"     // 2 cross arms for one pole
}

Now each cross arm get's two insulators beneath:

/sap/opu/odata4/sap/api_taskplanningelement/srvd_a2x/sap/taskplanningelement/0001/TaskPlanningElement/1539027/_TskPlngElmntBOMItem
{
  "MaintOrdOperationExecutionRate": 1,        //execution factor for operation processing
  "BillOfMaterialComponent": "1539028",       //insulator
  "NmbrOfCompatibleUnitInstances" : "2"       //2 insulator for one cross arm
}

You can verify the result by displaying the compatible unit structure list in transaction "Display Compatible Unit":

Figure 6: How to access the structure list - this icon is only visible for multi-level CUsFigure 7: Structure list of my example

 

Step 2.0 Create task planning design document

Use this request to create a new design document where you assign the pole planning element under the mandatory root structure element. To assign a planning element in the design hierarchy you need to define the node rank of the new element on your own. Use sequential numbers here , starting with 1. To define the parent element, you can either use the self-given parent node rank, or you can use the task planning assignment UUID in case the parent element already exists in the database.

/sap/opu/odata4/sap/api_taskplanningdesign/srvd_a2x/sap/taskplanningdesign/0001/TaskPlanningDesign
{
  "TaskPlanningDesignProfile": "0000000001", // use your own profile
  "TaskPlanningDesignType": "D",             // design single order use
  "MainWorkCenter": "EAM-ELEC",
  "TaskPlanningDesignDescription": "new connection – 50 classic poles",
  "_TskPlngElmntAssgmtDesign": 
[ {
      "TskPlngElmntAssgmtNodeRank": "0001"         // root structure element
    },
    {
      "TskPlngElmntAssgmtParNodeRank": "0001",     // root structure element
      "TskPlngElmntAssgmtNodeRank": "0002",        // pole planning element - node rank
      "TaskPlanningElementIdentifier": "1539025",  // pole planning element -id  
      "MaintOrdOperationExecutionRate" : 50        // we need 50 poles for the design
    }  ]
}

In the response, you receive eight planning element assignments: One root structure element, one pole, two cross arms, and four insulators in total.

{  // design document header 
  "@odata.context" :        
  "$metadata#TaskPlanningDesign(_TskPlngElmntAssgmtDesign())/$entity",
  "@odata.metadataEtag" : "W/\"20260212230942\"",
  "TaskPlanningDesignUUID" : "42010aef-acab-1fe1-8482-b858d8020a0e",
  "TaskPlanningDesignNumber" : "201568",
  "TaskPlanningDesignDescription" : "new connection - 50 utility poles",
  …
  ], 
  "_TskPlngElmntAssgmtDesign" : [  
    { // root structure element
      "TaskPlanningDesignUUID" : "42010aef-acab-1fe1-8482-b858d8020a0e",
      "TskPlngElmntAssgmtUUID" : "42010aef-acab-1fe1-8482-b858d8022a0e",
      "TaskPlanningElementIdentifier" : "",
      "TskPlngElmntAssgmtNodeRank" : "1",
      "TskPlngElmntAssgmtParNodeRank" : "0",
      "LastTskPlngElmntParentNodeRank" : "0",
      "SAP__Messages" : [  ]
    },
    { // task planning element: pole 
      "TaskPlanningDesignUUID" : "42010aef-acab-1fe1-8482-b858d8020a0e",
      "TskPlngElmntAssgmtUUID" : "42010aef-acab-1fe1-8482-b858d8024a0e",
      "TaskPlanningElementIdentifier" : "1539025",
      "TskPlngElmntAssgmtParNodeRank" : "0",
      "LastTskPlngElmntParentNodeRank" : "0",
      "TaskListGroupCounter" : "1",
      "TaskListGroup" : "POLE1",
      ...
   },
...
    {   // task planning element insulator
      "TaskPlanningDesignUUID" : "42010aef-acab-1fe1-8482-b858d8020a0e",
      "TskPlngElmntAssgmtUUID" : "42010aef-acab-1fe1-8485-619ba2b56a2b",
      "TaskPlanningElementIdentifier" : "1539028",
      "TskPlngElmntAssgmtDescription" : "insulator",
      "StructureElementDefaultType" : "",
      "TskPlngElmntAssgmtNodeRank" : "8",
      "TskPlngElmntAssgmtParNodeRank" : "6"
...
]

 

Step 2.1: Create value assignment for the utility pole

You now set the values for the mandatory value assignment of the pole like a user would do it manually in figure 3. In my example, this is characteristic "CU Action" and "CU Type". To address the pole in the design document you need to use the design UUID and the task planning assignment UUID of the pole from the previous response in the request URI. 

/sap/opu/odata4/sap/api_taskplanningdesign/srvd_a2x/sap/taskplanningdesign/0001/TskPlngElmntAssgmtDesign/42010aef-acab-1fe1-8485-619ba2b42a2b/42010aef-acab-1fe1-8485-619ba2b46a2b/SAP__self.CreateValueAssignment
{
  "_CharcValueComponent": [
    {
      "Characteristic" : "CU_ACTION",
      "CharcValue" : "INSTALL"
    },
    {
      "Characteristic" : "CU_TYPE",
      "CharcValue" : "POLE"
    }
  ]
}

 

Step 3.0 Create Construction Measure and release design

Create a construction measure:

/sap/opu/odata4/sap/api_constructionmeasure/srvd_a2x/sap/constructionmeasure/0001/ConstructionMeasure
{
  "ConstructionMeasureDescription": "New connection for SAP headquarter",
  "ConstructionMeasureType": "0002" //for internal numbering
}

Release the construction measure:

/sap/opu/odata4/sap/api_constructionmeasure/srvd_a2x/sap/constructionmeasure/0001/ConstructionMeasure/10841/SAP__self.Release

Update the design with the created construction measure (in my example it is 10841). Use here the PATCH-request:

/sap/opu/odata4/sap/api_taskplanningdesign/srvd_a2x/sap/taskplanningdesign/0001/TaskPlanningDesign/42010aef-acab-1fe1-8482-b858d8020a0e
{  "ConstructionMeasure": "10841"   }

 Release the design from the construction measure. Use here again the POST-request:

/sap/opu/odata4/sap/api_constructionmeasure/srvd_a2x/sap/constructionmeasure/0001/ConstructionMeasure/10841/SAP__self.SelectForRelease
{  "TaskPlanningDesignNumber": "201568"    }

 

Verify your result: Assign CU-Design in WebDynpro app "Process maintenance order"

Create a maintenance order with a order type which is enabled for Compatible Units. Choose the action "Assign CU Design" at the operation data tab of the maintenance order and select the CU design you released in the previous step. The operations for the "Install" CU-Action are determined and exploded into the operation list. You can still see the link between operation and CU at the order item level. This becomes important if you want to track costs on operational accounting level (OAA).

Figure 8: Assign CU-Design 201568 in a maintenance order

 

Further Links

  • Task Planning Management on help.sap.com here
  • App feature on help.sap.com here
  • https://api.sap.com/api/sap-s4-OP_TASKPLANNINGELEMENT_0001-v1
  • https://api.sap.com/api/sap-s4-OP_TASKPLANNINGDESIGN_0001-v1
  • https://api.sap.com/api/sap-s4-OP_CONSTRUCTIONMEASURE_0001-v1

entry.title: Traditional Preventive Maintenance(BJ2) Vs Phase Based Proactive Maintenance(4HI)
entry.content:
Comparison Between Preventive and Proactive Maintenance

 

Maintenance strategies are essential for enhancing equipment reliability, minimizing downtime, and managing operational expenses. Two of the most commonly adopted methods are preventive maintenance and proactive maintenance. Although the terms are occasionally confused, they signify distinct philosophies and varying levels of advancement in maintenance management. This blog examines the primary differences between preventive and proactive maintenance, their advantages, drawbacks, and appropriate contexts for their implementation.

 

What Is Preventive Maintenance?
Preventive maintenance is a planned, time-based maintenance strategy. Regardless of the actual state of the equipment, tasks like servicing, lubrication, inspections, and part replacements are carried out at predetermined intervals.

Key Features of Preventive Maintenance:
 Aims to reduce the likelihood of failure
 Reactive to known wear patterns
 Easy to plan and implement
 Reduces unexpected breakdowns
 Extends equipment lifespan compared to reactive maintenance
 Improves safety and compliance

Limitations:
 May result in needless maintenance
 Does not address root causes of failures
 May still allow unexpected breakdowns
 May increase labour and spare-part costs

Preventive Flow

 

 

 

 

 

What Is Proactive Maintenance?

Proactive maintenance focuses on eliminating the root causes of equipment failure before problems occur. Instead of asking when maintenance should be performed, it asks why failures happen and how they can be prevented permanently.

Key Characteristics of Proactive Maintenance

Condition-based and data-driven

Uses root cause analysis (RCA)

Relies on monitoring tools (vibration analysis, oil analysis, thermography)

Emphasizes design and process improvements

Advantages

Reduces recurring failures

Minimizes downtime and maintenance costs

Improves long-term reliability

Supports continuous improvement

Limitations

Higher upfront cost

Requires skilled personnel and advanced tools

More complex to implement

Needs organizational commitment

 

Proactive Flow

 

Phase Based Process – Proactive Maintenance

The Phase Based Process is divided into distinct steps which eases the end-user to view the order/notification in a granular view.

Each Phase has its own subphases/sub steps in Proactive maintenance that are mentioned below:

  1. Preparation
  2. Scheduling
  3. Execution
  4. Post-Execution
  5. Completion

 

Preparation:

Once an order is approved for execution and released, it is set to In Preparation and passed to the Preparation phase.

In this phase, the maintenance planner divides the work, resources, spare parts & services required.

By utilising the Manage Maintenance Planning Buckets Fiori application the planner segregates the work orders/planned shutdown activities based upon the type/duration of the maintenance process. Once orders are assigned to planning bucket scope, the list of orders can be reviewed with Manage Maintenance Backlog application.

This provides a detailed scope on the orders like consumables, non-stock components, resources & services

Once the maintenance planner is confident that all the required resources will be available on the required dates, they can select individual orders, choose Submit for Scheduling and thereby pass them on to the Scheduling phase.

 

 

 

 

Scheduling:

During this phase, the planner or supervisor evaluates the right work center is allocated to the right orders and time.

Note: The Operation Dispatch can also be done by Resource Scheduling. If MRS is excluded from the scope, then the operations/orders must be dispatched manually for collective or individual orders.

 

 

 

 

Check out how SAP MRS is used for Dispatching operations: Introducing the Phase Model

 

Execution:

The operations dispatched by the planner or supervisor are visible in Perform Maintenance Jobs application to the technician, who performs the assigned task and record time confirmation.

Upon successfully executing the jobs, the operations are set to subphase Work Finished.

 

 

 

Post Execution:

Post the time confirmation of proposed tasks with recommendations proposed. The order subphase is set to Main Work Completed which enables the Post Execution phase using Find Maintenance Order/Change Maintenance Order application.

Once the tasks are completed, services PR’s, stock components are utilised in the maintenance order, supervisor evaluates the maintenance cost, failure data and set the work order to Technically complete.

 

 

Completion:

Once the maintenance order is set to technically complete, all the postings related to order are closed including assigned notification(s) which are set to completion state.

During this phase, the financial controller reviews the maintenance order and settle the cost against the maintenance order and now the maintenance order is set to its final status Closed (Order) subphase.

 

 

 

Note: Furthermore, the Maintenance Planner/supervisor/Manager can analyse the cost and damages due to which the maintenance has been triggered using Maintenance Order Cost & Technical Object Damages Fiori application.

 

                  

 

 

 

 

 

Preventive vs Proactive Maintenance: Key Differences

Aspect

Preventive Maintenance

Proactive Maintenance

Approach

Planned maintenance

Planned maintenance

Focus

Preventing failure

Eliminating root causes

Effectiveness

Moderate

High

Long-Term Reliability

Improved

Optimized

Sub-Phases

Not included

Included

Application

GUI tiles

Enhanced web dynpro application



When to Use Each Strategy

  • Preventive maintenance is ideal for:
    • Organizations with limited resources
    • Non-critical equipment
    • Early stages of maintenance maturity
  • Proactive maintenance is best for:
    • Critical assets
    • High-cost downtime environments
    • Organizations aiming for operational excellence

In reality, a lot of businesses employ a hybrid strategy, beginning with preventive maintenance and progressively incorporating proactive methods as knowledge, resources, and skills increase.

Conclusion
     Proactive maintenance goes a step further by addressing the underlying causes of equipment problems, whereas preventive maintenance reduces failures through planned activities. While proactive maintenance offers better long-term reliability and cost savings, preventive maintenance is simpler to implement and provides immediate benefits. Budget, organizational maturity, and asset criticality all play a role in selecting the best approach, or a combination of the two.

entry.title: What's New in SAP Asset Performance Management 2602
entry.content:
The 2602 release of SAP Asset Performance Management introduces enhancements across Asset Data & Information Management, Asset Reliability Engineering, and Asset Health Monitoring.

This release includes improvements such as smarter notification replication between SAP ERP and APM, expanded industrial system modeling, extensible and time-aware FMEA assessments, and streamlined rule execution. Together, these updates help reduce manual effort while improving data clarity, structural transparency, and lifecycle control.

Let’s take a closer look.

Asset Data and Information Management

New Filters for Maintenance Notification Replication Between SAP ERP and APM.

A reliability engineer starts the day by reviewing maintenance notifications from SAP ERP.

The challenge

Until now, all notifications were replicated into the APM system, including urgent failures, minor inspections, test notifications, and even items irrelevant for reliability analysis. This created unnecessary noise, increased data volumes, and required manual effort to identify what truly mattered. Ensuring consistent handling across SAP ERP and the APM system added further complexity.

The solution

With enhanced filtering capabilities in the Communication System configuration, notification management becomes more intelligent and controlled.

Notification-type filters can now be defined so that only relevant notifications are replicated to the APM system. Critical maintenance issues are routed directly to reliability and performance analysis, while operational or non-relevant notifications remain in ERP.

Communication System configuration including filters for maintenance notifications

The result

  • Reliability engineers work with clean, focused notification data
  • Unnecessary data transfer between systems is reduced
  • Notifications are handled consistently across SAP ERP and the APM system

The same filtering logic also applies to APM alerts and Rules that generate notifications back to SAP ERP. Only active filters are available during notification creation and rule configuration, ensuring governed and consistent integration in both directions.

By the time trend and failure pattern analysis begins, the data is already curated — eliminating manual filtering and second-guessing.

Configuration is maintained directly within the Communication Systems settings, ensuring that the right notifications reach the right system to support efficient and reliable maintenance decisions.

Watch the video to see it in action.

Watch the video: How to set filters to ensure only relevant notifications are replicated

Discover Industrial System Associations via OData API

Understanding how technical objects relate to your industrial system is essential for transparency, integration, and advanced analytics.

To support this, the Technical Object OData API has been enhanced to expose industrial system relationships directly within the service.

What’s new

The API schema now includes two new associations:

  • Industrial System Perspective
  • Industrial System Mapping

These additions allow consumers of the API to retrieve and clearly identify how technical objects are associated within the industrial system structure.

Why this matters

With industrial system relationships now accessible through the API:

  • Integration scenarios become more transparent
  • External applications can better interpret system context
  • Reporting and analytics can incorporate industrial structure information
  • Custom extensions and automation scenarios gain richer structural insight

This enhancement improves data discoverability and makes system relationships accessible in a standardized, API-driven way. For more information, see the official documentation for the Explore Technical Objects API.

SAP Business Accelerator Hub in the Schema View of the “Explore Technical Objects” API

Industrial Systems - Doubling Capacity for Complex Systems

The challenge

As industrial environments grow, so does their complexity. Large plants, distributed equipment landscapes, and deeply structured functional locations require detailed modeling to ensure transparency, traceability, and performance management.

Until now, System Elements were limited to 1,000 Technical Objects. For smaller setups, this worked well. But in enterprise-scale environments, that ceiling quickly became a bottleneck.

The solution

We have doubled the previous assignment capability. You can now:

  • Assign up to 2000 technical objects per System Element for the Plant system perspective
  • Assign up to 2000 technical objects per System Element for Functional Location and Equipment perspective.

The result

  • Improved scalability to support large and complex industrial environments.
  • Increased customer satisfaction by supporting enterprise-scale industrial use cases.

Asset Reliability Engineering

FMEA Assessments: Now transitioned to the Extensible SAP Cloud Application Programming (CAP) Model

The challenge

As of today, the Manage FMEA Assessments application does not support field extensibility, timeline changes, or standard sort and search capabilities across its list and object detail views. This limits flexibility and makes it difficult for organizations to tailor assessments to their specific industry or business requirements. Managing and navigating assessments can become inefficient, especially when customization or structured data handling is required.

The solution

To address these limitations, we introduced a new FMEA Assessment application based on the SAP Cloud Application Programming Model (CAP) and OData APIs. The new application provides out-of-the-box support for extensibility, enabling users to add custom fields and adapt the application to industry-specific requirements and data capture needs.

During a transition period, the new application will gradually replace the current one, providing a modern and scalable foundation for assessment management.

Object page in the FMEA CAP-based application and new tile from SAP Fiori Launchpad

The result

With centralized handling and built-in extensibility, organizations gain greater flexibility and control over their FMEA assessments. The enhanced capabilities support customized data structures and industry-specific adaptations, while also delivering an improved user experience with more intuitive, consistent, and efficient assessment management.

To use this application, you must have the role FMEAAssessment_CAP assigned to your user.

For more information, refer to Manage FMEA (Failure Mode and Effects Analysis) Assessments .

SAP Help Portal  

For more information on release timelines, implementation, support, and known limitations, refer to SAP Note 3603407

Define Validity Periods for FMEA assessments

You can now define validity periods for FMEA assessments

The challenge

FMEA assessments are not static documents — they evolve over time. However, without structured validity control, it can be difficult to clearly define when an assessment becomes effective or when it expires.

This lack of time-based governance can lead to confusion around which version of an assessment is currently valid, especially when assets, operating conditions, or risk evaluations change. Managing assessment lifecycle and traceability without clear validity periods reduces transparency and can impact compliance and audit readiness.

The solution

The enhanced FMEA Assessment application introduces structured validity date management. Users can define “Valid From” and “Valid To” dates directly within the assessment using an intuitive calendar control.

This enables clear lifecycle control of each assessment and ensures that time-dependent changes are properly documented. Validity dates allow organizations to manage transitions between assessment versions in a controlled and transparent way, supporting structured updates and future-dated planning.

Validity section in the FMEA assessment object page

The result

With defined validity periods, FMEA assessments become time-aware and easier to govern.

Organizations gain:

  • Clear visibility into which assessment version is currently active
  • Improved traceability of historical and future assessments
  • Better lifecycle management and compliance support

By integrating validity control directly into the application, FMEA assessments are no longer static records but governed, time-based reliability artifacts.

 

Asset Health Monitoring

Rerun Failed Rules for Technical Objects

The challenge

Rule-based automation plays a central role in maintaining data quality and process consistency across technical objects.

When a rule fails, however, automation slows down. Failed executions can lead to inconsistencies, follow-up effort, and delays in downstream processes (especially in large industrial environments managing high volumes of data).

Without a direct way to rerun failed rules, restoring process stability required additional steps and manual intervention.

The solution

The View Rule Logs application now includes a Rerun Rules button, allowing users to rerun failed rules for technical objects immediately. This enables fast recovery directly within the process with no workaround and no disruption.

Access to this capability is governed by the new Rules_Execute role:

  • Currently, the function works without explicit role assignment
  • Starting with release 2608, users must have the Rules_Execute role to use it

This ensures operational flexibility today and strengthened governance going forward.

New "Rerun Rules" button in the View Rule Logs application

The result

With this enhancement, organizations gain:

  • Faster resolution of failed rule executions
  • Reduced manual correction effort
  • Improved data consistency and reliability
  • Clear authorization control over rule re-execution

For more information, see View Rule Logs.

 

Stay Informed and Get Involved

  • If you missed them, check out our recent roadmap and What's New 2602 (incl. 2510-2601) webcasts
  • You can also explore our latest blog posts here, including topics such as Embedded IoT by @florian_weigold, Industrial Systems by @SonamKinger, and the extensibility blog by @archigarg.
  • Find out what's up and coming in the SAP Road Map Explorer.
  • For an exhaustive list of all new features, visit the SAP Help Portal - What's New in SAP Asset Performance Management.
  • Send us your improvement requests. Submit or vote for the improvement you would like to see in the SAP Continuous Influence session.
  • Check the January update of the APM link collection. 

See you for the 2603 update!

entry.title: SAP Asset Performance Management Meets SAP Situation Handling - Extended Framework
entry.content:
In this blog, I will outline how the SAP Situation Handling Extended Framework can be leveraged to notify Master Data Specialist when Maintenance Planner creates a maintenance objects such as Task Lists, Maintenance Plans, while implementing SAP Asset Performance Management Recommendations.

Business Value:

  • Operational Efficiency - Automating notifications eliminates manual follow-ups and reduces delays in master data validation.
  • Improved Collaboration and Transparency - This integration reduces communication gaps and ensures that master data is aligned with operational needs.
  • Optimized maintenance strategies extend asset life cycles and lower total cost of ownership.

If you want to gain a deep understanding of SAP Situation Handling - Standard Framework and SAP Intelligent Scenario Lifecycle Management, dive into my latest blog posts mentioned below.

  • S/4HANA Intelligent Scenario Lifecycle Management (ISLM) and Situation Handling Scenarios: Part-1 
  • S/4HANA Intelligent Scenario Lifecycle Management (ISLM) and Situation Handling Scenarios: Part-2  

let us have a look at the high level process flow of how SAP APM Recommendations integrate with SAP Situation Handling Extended Framework.

SAP APM Recommendations:

SAP Situation Handling Extended Framework:

Also, If you want to gain knowledge on SAP Signavio Process Manager & Process Collaboration Hub, please check out my blog post mentioned below. This blog provides a broad outline of process modelling.

  • The nuts and bolts of a well-thought-out SAP Signavio : Process Manager & Process Collaboration Hub 

My Business Use Case:

In my organization, maintenance planners operationalize the SAP APM Recommendations through maintenance task list and maintenance plans. The master data specialists team ensures governance by monitoring these processes through automated notifications enabled by Situation Handling Extended Framework.

Let us first focus on the key concepts of "Situation Handling - Extended Framework" and setup a situation type-extended for a maintenance object: Task List.

Situation Object: Technical model of a business object used in situation handling. This includes CDS views, navigation targets, events, and actions.

Situation Scenario: Business scenario that builds on situation objects. There are two kinds of situation scenarios. 

  • Object-based: Business scenario that builds on situation objects such as a Task List or a Maintenance Plan or a Maintenance Item.
  • Message-based: Business scenario that builds on system messages  from run types and reports.

As per my business use case, we will be exploring object-based situation scenarios listed below.

Situation Template: Template serves as a blueprint for creating a situation type.

Situation Type: Description of a business situation. For example, how a situation instance is triggered and who is informed about it.

Situation Instance: Instance of an exceptional issue which is displayed to the end user. It is generated according to the settings of the enabled situation type:

  • For Object-based situations: Whenever the conditions defined for the situation type are fulfilled.
  • For Message-based situations: Based on the run type settings for scheduled application jobs.

When situation instances occur, specified end users see the situations in the "My Situations-Extended" app, and also receive notifications on SAP FLP, Emails.

In order to get started with the Situation Handling Extended Framework, we must first define "Responsibility Management" for our object-based situation scenarios.

Intelligent systems need to determine agents(Responsible Persons) who are automatically notified about events and who are responsible for business processes and objects. 

The predefined settings in the situation template determine that the notification addresses only members of the "MAINT_MGMT" team category.

Team Information:

Responsibility Definitions and Member Function options allow us to select further filters  to determine the users responsible. For Instance, let us choose Main Work Center, Maintenance Planner Group, Maintenance Planning Plant, and Maintenance Plant.

Team Owner and Team Members:

Once we have enabled the Responsibility Team, we can proceed for  configuring the situation types-extended.

 

SAP provides the templates as listed below.

For demonstration purpose, I have used the EAM_MAINTENANCE_TASK_LIST template and created a Situation Type-Extended with an ID : Z_CAL_MAINT_TASK_LIST - Calcination Maintenance Task List.

we can provide the header information and situation instances criteria. However, the navigational actions are predefined with respect to each situation type.

The navigation actions are navigation options to business apps that can help users to solve a situation. In our business use case, we have "Find Maintenance Task List" app as a navigation action.

Now, let us define the conditions,

Mini Note: When we create a Task List via SAP APM Recommendation as part of Implement Recommendations, system creates a task list with status "1-Created" and assigns respective planning plant. So, let us consider the fields that SAP APM considers while creating a Task List.

and Batch job Scheduling parameters.

A situation Instance is created when the overall status of a maintenance task list is "Created"  and situation/notification is displayed as per the predefined text template.

Now let us assign the team that was created in the earlier step.

Now let us enable the Situation Type.

Now, let us perform FMEA(Failure Mode and Effects Analysis) assessment and create a "Task List" for one of the FMEA recommendations.

For Instance, I have performed an FMEA on Equipment 10001730 (Reactor 101 DIN2816 AIE). Based on the resulting RPN score, a recommendation was created/reviewed/released for Implementation.

FMEA Assessment:

Creating a Recommendation:

Let us now use "Manage Recommendations" app to update, review, and release the recommendation.

Review:

Ready for Release:

Released:

Once the recommendations are released, maintenance planners can initiate the implementation process.

Implement Recommendations:

As part of the recommendation implementation process, maintenance planners have to update the status of the recommendation to "Implementation In Process".

Once the status becomes "Implementation In Process", the maintenance planner can either assign the existing task list and maintenance plan or create a new task list and maintenance plan.

Since there is no task list exists in S/4HANA for this recommendation, let us create a new task list for this recommendation.

Now, as per the conditions maintained in the Situation Type "Z_CAL_MAINT_TASK_LIST", the system triggered the notifications.

Monitor Situations - Extended app helps us monitor situations created by the extended framework for situation handling. Monitoring Situation instances is enabled by default. We can display the activities performed for situation instances using different chart types and also as a table. To capture and show monitoring data, we must need to enable "Monitor Instances" while configuring the situation type-extended.

Let us first check the Monitor Situations - Extended app to visualize the situation instances that have been created so far.

Now, let us view the Notifications.

Option-1: We can view the notifications using "Bell Icon" on FLP screen.

Options-2: We can view notifications using "My Situations-Extended" app. Using this app, we can view all the situations as per the responsibility area. 

 

Scenario Selection Area: The list view shows all situations. We can go to situation page for further information or close the situation directly in the list view.

Situation Page: The situation page displays the detail information of a situation as of the point in time it was created, however, this can differ from the current date.

The option "Assign To Me" : We can assign a situation to ourselves. Once we assign to ourselves, then the system updates processor details with our name. If we stop working on this situation, we can unassign ourselves and the respective situation is displayed as "Open".

Now let us use the "Find Maintenance Task List" app to update the task list operations.

"Find Maintenance Task List" app will navigate us to the "Change Task List" app.

Let us now update the required operations for the task list group 36 and counter 1.

Once the respective Task List is updated, we can close the situation.

😊This brings me to the end of my process walk through on how we can leverage SAP Situation Handling Extended Framework for SAP Asset Performance Management (APM) - Implement Recommendations. I hope this blog proves useful to the Maintenance Management Team. I look forward to the adoption of the Situation Handling Extended Framework as part of SAP APM implementation to improve operational efficiency.

 

--

Yours Sincerely,

Pardhasaradhi Reddy. Chelikam

entry.title: Maintenance Plan - Mass Scheduling by Using Multiple Items
entry.content:
Problem Statement

In the current maintenance planning process, mass scheduling of Maintenance Plans (MP) is determined based on the start of cycle date. Once the start of the cycle is set, it cannot be modified for the purpose of mass scheduling start date determination.

Although individual maintenance plans or call dates can be adjusted using the New Start function in IP10, this adjustment must be performed plan by plan, which becomes inefficient when dealing with multiple related plans.

This limitation creates challenges when rescheduling groups of maintenance activities, particularly for turnaround (TA)-related maintenance tasks, where multiple plans often need to be shifted together.

Proposed Solution

To improve flexibility and simplify rescheduling, multiple maintenance items can be grouped under a single maintenance plan.

By creating multiple maintenance items within one maintenance plan, the entire group of related activities can be manually rescheduled using the “New Start” function in IP10.

This approach enables the following:

  • Grouping of Turnaround (TA)-related maintenance tasks
  • Simplified rescheduling using a single plan
  • Reduced manual effort compared to adjusting multiple individual plans
  • Better synchronization of maintenance activities

With this setup, TA-related maintenance activities can be organized as multiple maintenance items under one maintenance plan, allowing efficient and coordinated rescheduling when required.

• Maintenance plan mass scheduling is done based on the start of cycle date set in the maintenance plan. IP30 Deadline Monitoring or IP30H Mass Schedule Maintenance Plans

• IP30H automatically performs a preselection of due maintenance plans, which significantly reduces runtime.

• It allows users to schedule all maintenance plans that are due within a specific timeframe in one step.

• Optimized for SAP S/4HANA, leveraging server group and parallel process configuration to handle large data sets quickly.

• Parameters: Users can define the timeframe (number of days) and choose a server group with the number of parallel processes for improved speed.

• Log: A scheduling log can be displayed using transaction SLG1. 

Maintenance plan mass scheduling is done based on the start of cycle date set in the maintenance plan. IP30 Deadline Monitoring or IP30H Mass Schedule Maintenance Plans

Multiple maintenance items that are relevant for turnaround can be grouped in one maintenance plan, which will allow manual rescheduling as per the new start date from IP10. This way, the TA-turnaround-related maintenance plans can be grouped and created as multiple maintenance items against one plan.

Key Takeaway

🔹Start of Cycle controls mass scheduling in maintenance plans and is not easily changed once set.
🔹IP10 “New Start” allows rescheduling but is inefficient when handling many individual plans.
🔹Grouping multiple maintenance items under one maintenance plan enables faster and coordinated rescheduling.
🔹This approach significantly simplifies Turnaround (TA) maintenance planning and improves scheduling efficiency in SAP EAM.

#SAP #SAPEAM #SAPPM #EnterpriseAssetManagement #MaintenancePlanning #PreventiveMaintenance

entry.title: Driving Efficiency with SAP S/4HANA 2025 Reactive Maintenance(4HH)
entry.content:
The Reactive Maintenance Process Flow (4HH)

  1. Initiation & Screening: A notification is created for a breakdown, then screened/accepted.
  2. Planning & Approval: The request is converted into a maintenance order, with resources and parts planned and approved.
  3. Preparation & Scheduling: Parts are ordered, and the work is scheduled.
  4. Execution & Completion: Work is performed, confirmed via Fiori apps, and technically completed. 

2025 Key Updates & Best Practices

  • Best Practice 4HH: SAP strongly recommends adopting the new 4HH scope item, which has replaced older best practices (BH1, BH2, BJ2).
  • "Completed in Addition" Status: When operating in resource scheduling (e.g., using the "Manage Schedule" app), maintenance jobs that are added after the schedule is frozen are tracked as "Completed in Addition."
  • Emergency Order Handling: The "Notification Processing Context" field identifies emergency work, allowing for immediate assignment to technicians.
  • Mobile Capability: Maintenance requests and actions can be executed via mobile apps

    Phases and Subphases

System : S4HANA 2025

Script : Reactive Maintenance (4HH)

Configure Flexible Workflow for Maintenance Orders

Step 1. Log on : Login with Business Role Configuration Expert 

Step 2. Access the App : Fiori App—Manage Workflows for Maintenance Orders—Asset Management


Step 3. Check Automatic Approval for Maintenance Order

Step 2: Access the App; Fiori App – Manage Maintenance Planning Buckets Old Version (F3888)



Step 3. Create New Planning Bucket

Step 2: Access the App; Fiori App – Create Maintenance Request (F1511A)


Step 3. Enter Details

Step 4. Assess Priority

entry.title: Get Help Where You Need it: SAP Companion in SAP Asset Performance Management
entry.content:
Access relevant help, documentation, and guidance directly within your workflow
Daily work in SAP Asset Performance Management often involves navigating different applications, reviewing information, and completing operational tasks. During this process, users sometimes want quick clarification about a feature, an application purpose, or the next step in a workflow.

Now imagine being able to access that guidance without leaving the application you are working in. The information appears within the same screen, aligned with the application you are currently using, allowing you to continue your work while exploring helpful context.

This is where SAP Companion adds value. It brings contextual guidance directly into the application interface so that users can access relevant information exactly when they need it.

What is SAP Companion

SAP Companion is an in-application assistance feature that provides contextual help within SAP applications.
The guidance appears as a help panel overlay on top of the active application screen, offering relevant guidance based on the page or application you are currently using.
This approach supports a natural learning experience because users can learn while they work, accessing information exactly when they need it.

Why Contextual Help Is Valuable

Providing help in the right context improves the user experience and reduces the effort required to find information. SAP Companion in SAP Asset Performance Management offers several benefits:
  • Context-Sensitive Help: Users receive information relevant to the task or application they are currently working on.
  • Stay in Your Workflow: Help appears directly within the interface, so users do not need to navigate away from the application.
  • Faster Onboarding: New users can quickly understand application functionality while performing their daily tasks.
  • Improved Productivity: Users can resolve questions instantly without searching through large documentation portals.

Accessing SAP Companion

Accessing contextual help is simple.

  1. Open the Fiori Launchpad.

  2. In the launchpad header, select the Help icon.

  3. The help panel opens and displays contextual information related to your current page or application.

The help panel appears as an overlay on your current screen, allowing you to continue working while accessing relevant guidance.

Help icon in the application header used to access in-app assistance (SAP companion).

Information Available in the Help Panel

The help panel offers several types of information designed to support users directly within their workflow.

  • Contextual Snippets: Short descriptions introduce the purpose and main functionality of the application. These snippets provide quick orientation and help users understand what the application is designed for.
  • Direct Links to Documentation: Each application includes a link that leads directly to its guide in the SAP Help Portal, allowing users to explore detailed documentation whenever needed.
  • Technical Component Details: The help panel also displays the technical application component identifier. This information can be useful when creating support tickets or communicating with support teams.

Contextual help snippet showing the explanatory text, a link to documentation, and the technical component ID.

Watch the Demo

To see how contextual help works in practice, watch the video below:

 
Video demonstrating how to access in-app assistance.

Guidance that appears directly within the application interface creates a more connected learning experience for users. 

With SAP Companion, contextual assistance becomes part of the user experience in SAP Asset Performance Management, ensuring that help is always available exactly where it is needed.

By delivering the right information at the right moment, SAP Companion helps users work more efficiently and confidently within the application.

For detailed information about the application and how to use its feature, refer to the application guide:
SAP Asset Performance Management | SAP Help Portal

To help shape future enhancements, you can share your feedback and vote for features through the SAP Customer Influence Portal. Your input directly contributes to improving the experience.
